oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Evaluation of the shed and spread of infectious bovine rhinotracheitis, parainfluenza-3 and bovine respiratory syncytial virus in young calves post-intranasal vaccination
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Bromm, Jenna J.
Cull, Charley A.
Lechtenberg, Kelly F.
Singu, Vijay K.
Seagren, Jon E.
Amachawadi, Raghavendra G.
Swafford, William S.
Braun, Douglas A.
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: BRSV
IBR
PI3
Histophilus somni
vaccine
intranasal
neonatal
cattle
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Intranasal vaccines are critical for early prevention of re­spiratory diseases in neonatal calves; however, concerns remain regarding potential transmission of vaccine virus strains to unvaccinated commingled animals, particularly in high-stress environments where vaccination history may be unknown. This study evaluated the presence, duration and shedding of infectious bovine rhinotracheitis (IBR), parainflu­enza virus type 3 (PI3), and bovine respiratory syncytial virus (BRSV) in nasal secretions of intranasally vaccinated calves and transmission to commingled unvaccinated controls. Three commercially available intranasal vaccines, Inforce 3® (Zoetis), Nasalgen® 3-PMH (Merck), and PrimeLine® IN5 (Huvepharma), were evaluated. Holstein-cross, mixed-sex, colostrum-deprived calves, seronegative and PCR-negative for IBR, PI3 and BRSV, were enrolled. Blood samples were col­lected on days 0 and 21 to measure antibody titers using viral serum neutralization assays. Nasal swabs were collected daily from days 0 to 21 and analyzed by PCR for IBR, PI3 and BRSV. Nasal swabs were also tested for Histophilus somni by PCR to assess prevalence. Calves were randomly assigned to 4 groups housed in separate biosafety level 2 rooms. Each vaccine group contained commingled, inoculated and non-inoculated calves. A fourth sentinel group was monitored for wild-type virus or biosecurity breaches. Intranasal vaccination resulted in detectable virus in nasal secretions and transmission to commingled non-inoculated calves. Seroconversion in inocu­lated and non-inoculated calves confirmed vaccine virus shed­ding and pen-level spread. These findings demonstrate that intranasal vaccine strains can complicate diagnostic interpre­tation during respiratory disease investigations, underscoring the importance of vaccination history when distinguishing vaccine-derived from wild-type viruses in clinical and research settings.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: The Bovine Practitioner; Vol. 60 No. 2 (2026): Online First; 32-40
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
